title: Stripping and oiling a garden chair
subtitle: Strip the old finish back to bare wood, sand through the grades, apply exterior oil
excerpt: How to restore a weathered teak or hardwood garden chair: stripping old finish with a paint scraper and stripper, sanding through progressively finer grits, and applying exterior wood oil to restore protection and colour.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Ronseal and Osmo exterior wood finish guides (publicly available). Traditional woodfinishing guides.

recipeTools.notes: For heavily weathered wood with deep checking; skip this grit on lightly weathered chairs.
Required only if the chair has a painted or lacquered finish to remove; bare teak that has only grayed does not need chemical stripping.

glossaryTerms.definition: A penetrating oil finish for outdoor hardwood. Unlike a surface film (varnish or lacquer), oil soaks into the wood fibre and does not peel or flake as the wood moves. Requires re-application every one to two years.

Fine surface cracks in wood caused by repeated wet-dry cycles during weathering. The wood surface shrinks and cracks as moisture leaves. Surface checking is cosmetic on hardwood garden furniture; checking that goes deep into the grain indicates more serious degradation.

content.text: Hardwood garden furniture (teak, iroko, oak) grays and checks on exposure but rarely degrades structurally for many years. Restoring it involves stripping any remaining old finish, sanding back to clean wood, and applying a fresh coat of
. Work outdoors or in a well-ventilated area; old finishes produce dust and stripper produces vapour.
Step 1, assess and strip the old finish

If the chair has a surface film (painted, lacquered, or varnished), apply water-based paint stripper generously, wait 30 to 60 minutes, and scrape off with the paint scraper. Work with the grain. Repeat on areas where the finish is thick. Wash off the residue with clean water and allow to dry fully (at least one day).
If the chair is already bare but weathered gray, scraping alone is enough: scrape along the grain to lift the loose gray surface layer.
Start with 80-grit if the wood has deep

or stubborn old finish; otherwise start with 120-grit. Sand with the grain at each grit, using a sanding block on flat surfaces. Move to 180-grit for the final pass. The wood should be uniformly pale and smooth with no visible scratches from the previous grit. Wipe off all dust with a dry cloth before moving to the next grit.
Step 3, apply the first coat of oil

Apply exterior wood oil liberally with a brush or lint-free cloth, working it into the end grain and all joints first (these absorb most oil). Brush out any puddles and wipe off any excess after 15 to 20 minutes. The first coat on bare wood absorbs fully and may look dry immediately; this is correct. Allow to dry for the period specified on the tin (typically 6 to 24 hours).

Lightly abrade the surface with 000 steel wool or 240-grit sandpaper to cut back any raised grain. Wipe off the dust. Apply a second coat of oil and again wipe off any surplus after 20 minutes. Two coats are the minimum on bare wood. A third coat on end grain and joints improves durability.

attrs.cause: Excess oil was not wiped off and has polymerised on the surface rather than in the wood.
attrs.fix: Rub the sticky area with a cloth dampened with white spirit to dissolve the surface oil layer. Allow to dry and re-apply a thin coat, wiping off all surplus within 20 minutes.

attrs.symptom: Wood looks patchy after the first coat
attrs.cause: Some areas were more absorbent than others (often around joints or where the old finish was thinner).
attrs.fix: Apply additional oil to the dull or flat areas after the first coat has dried, then apply the second coat over the whole surface.
